text: BAVARIA (UPPER), an administrative division of the above kingdom, nearly corresponding to the Isar circle in the former arrangement. It has an area of 311.14 German, or 6,607 English sq. m. Pop. in 1817, 489,452; in 1846, 705,544, of whom 660,618 were Catholics, and 10,147 Protestants. In 1837 there were 15 towns, 41 boroughs, 3,227 villages, and about 8,000 hamlets and isolated farms, in the district. The southern part of this circle is surrounded by the Noric Alps, and presents a rugged scene of mountains and valleys. The highest mountains of Bavaria—the Zugspitz, 9,689 ft.,—the Wetter Schrofen, 9,387 ft.,—the Muhlsteinhern, 7,000 ft.,—and the Staufen near Reichenhall, 5,408 ft.—are situated here. The rest of the circle is flat; but has large forests and mosses. The principal river is the Isar, a rapid stream which runs through the middle of the circle; among its tributaries are the Loisach and the Amper. The second large river is the Inn, to which belong the Salza, the Manzfall, the Aicha, and the Saala. The Lech is likewise a considerable river. This circle abounds in lakes, the most remarkable of which are the Chiemsee, covering an area of 22,500 acres, -the Ammersee, about half that size, -and the Tegernsee which is 50 fathoms deep. The great wealth of this district consists in its forests; the rearing of cattle is also carried on to some extent in the mountainous districts. The mines are valuable, particularly those of salt; iron and coals are also wrought. Munich, the capital of the kingdom and residence of the king, and one of the handsomest cities in Germany, is situated in this circle on the Isar, on a plain 1,920 ft. above the sea. It contained in 1846, 113,384 inhabitants.

BAVAY, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Nord.—The cant. comprises 18 com. Pop. in 1831, 13,420.—The com. and town are 15 m. NNW of Avesnes. Pop. 1,635. The town is on the site of the Bagacum or Baganum of the Romans, which was the cap. of the Nervii to the close of the 4th cent.; and marked the junction of several Roman roads.
BAVENO, a town of Sardinia, in the prov. and 2 m. WSW of Pallanza, on the r. bank of the Lago Maggiore, on the Simplon road.
BAVENT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Calvados, cant. of Troarn. Pop. 968.

BAVERSTOCK, a parish in Wiltshire, 4 m. W of Wilton, on the line of the South-Western railway. Area 710 acres. Pop. 194. BAVI, VAFI, or VAFKAND, a river of Bokhara, rising in the Nura Tau, and passing by Soghd, Samarcand, and Bokhara, towards the Oxus, which, in some maps, it is made to join in N lat. 48° 35', but it appears rather to lose itself in an expanse of marsh, called Karakol, about 30 m. SW of Bokhara.
BAVIA, a town of Mexico, in the dep. of Cohahuila, 90 m. N by W of Monclava, on a tributary of the Rio Bravo del Norte.

BAVIAANS RIVER, a river of S Africa, in the territory of Somerset, rising in that portion of the Groen Bergen called Cheviot fells, and flowing, in a winding course SW by S through Glen Linden, to the Great Fish river, which it joins on the l. bank, in S lat. 32° 45'.
BAVIAANSBERG, a village of S Africa, in the co. of Clanwilliam, in the Onder Bokkeveld, 80 m. NE of Clanwilliam.
BAVILLIERS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Haut-Rhin, cant. and 11 m. SW of Belfort. Pop. 479.
BAVINCHOVE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nord, cant. of Cassel. Pop. 1,056.
BAVINCOURT, a commune of France, in the cant. of Avesnes-le-Comte. Pop. 523.

BAVINGTON (GREAT and LITTLE), two townships in Northumberland, the former in the p. of Kirkwhelpington, 11 m. N by E of Hexham; pop. 69; the latter, in the p. of Thockrington, 10 m. NNE of Hexham; pop. 91.
BAVISPE. See HUAQUI. BAVLU, BAULU, or BARLA, a lofty mountain of Anatolia, near Lake Egerdir. Its lower skirts are covered with plantations of grapes and white mulberries.
BAVONDA, or PAGONTUS, a village in the island of Samos, near the Imbrasus river.
BAW, or BAU, a town of Denmark, in Sleswick, in the bailiwick and 5 m. N of Flensborg.
BAWBURGH, a parish in Norfolk, 5 m. W of Norwich, on the Yare river. Area with Bowthorpe, 2,070 acres. Pop. with Bowthorpe, 438.
BAWDESWELL, a parish in Norfolk, 4 m. W of Reepham. Area 1,310 acres. Pop. 582.
BAWDRIP, a parish in Somersetshire, 3 m. NE of Bridgewater. Area 1,780 acres. Pop., exclusive of that of Bridgewater, 425.
BAWDSEY, a parish in Suffolk, 8 m. SE of Woodbridge. Area 2,640 acres. Pop. 468.
BAWIS, a powerful tribe of Cha'b Arabs, in the prov. of Kuzistan, on the r. and l. banks of the Karun, above and below Ismailiyah.
BAWNBOY, a hamlet in the p. of Templeport, co. Cavan, 5 m. S of Swanlinbar. Pop. 96.

BA-WULIMA, a large tributary of the Senegal, rising in the Manding territory, under the 8th meridian, flowing WNW, and uniting with the Kokoro, 50 m. above the junction of the united stream with the Senegal, on the r. bank.
BAWSEY, a parish in Norfolk, 2 m. E of King's Lynn. Area 1,090 acres. Pop. 28.

BAWTRY, a chapelry and market-town in the p. of Blyth, W. R. of Yorkshire, 9 m. SE of Doncaster. Area of c., 270 acres. Pop. 1,093. The town is near the Idle or Iredale, which is here navigable for small craft, and by means of which it conducts a considerable transit trade in stone, timber, lead, and iron.
BAXADA, a town of La Plata, the cap. of the prov. of Entre Rios, on the l. bank of the Parana, in N lat. 31° 44'. BAY land on the coast of Guinea, in N lat. 5° 40', between Mesurado and Cape Palmas.
BAXO, an island half-a-mile distant from the SW end of Porto Santo, in Madeira. It is 11 m. in length, and half-a-mile in breadth. Its geognostical structure is fully described by Bowdich.
BAXOS DE VILLA LOBOS, a group of low islets in the Pacific, between Mulgrave's archipelago and the Sandwich islands.
BAXOS (CAPE), a low point of land on the W coast of Africa, between Cape Formosa and Palmas island.
BAXTERLY, a parish in Warwickshire, 31 m. W of Atherstone. Area 850 acres. Pop. 228.
BAY OF BENGAL. See BENGAL; and generally see the distinguishing name added to the geographical term bay, for places beginning with this word. See also Bahla.
BAY, a township in Ottawa co., in the state of Ohio, U. S., on the W side of Sandusky river. Pop. 230.

BAY (LAKE), or LAGUNA DE BAHIA, a sheet of water in the island of Luçon, in the Philippines, 20 m. long, by 10 m. in breadth. It discharges its waters by the Rio Pasig into the Bahia de Manilla. Its surface is covered with islets, of which the largest is called Talem. It abounds in fish, and is infested by crocodiles, some of which attain a prodigious size. -Mallat.

BAYA, a water-course of Costa Rica, in Central America, which commences at the port of Moin, and runs parallel to the coast as far as Pearl Kay lagune, a distance of 180 m. It is from 20 to 25 yards wide, and boats can navigate it from Moin to the river Matina, and up that river to the Real, and thence to Chirripo or Barbilla. It is not ascertained whether this canal is a work of nature or of art.
BAYA-BAYA, a large and thriving town of Western Africa, between Wallia, the cap. of Benna, and Kambaya, the cap. of Talla. The Sierra Leone traders procure coffee, wax, hides, and rice here.
BAYAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Dordogne, cant. of Beaumont. Pop. 622.
BAYAD, a district of Anatolia, between Iskelib and Kankari, in N lat. 40° 30'.

BAYA'MO, or SAN SALVADOR DEL BAYAMO, a town in the SE part of the island of Cuba, in N lat. 20° 40′, near the l. bank of the Cauto. Pop. 7,000. Sugar and coffee are grown in some parts of the surrounding country, which also affords excellent pasturage. The harbour of Manzanillo is the port of B.
BAYAN DZIROUKE, a mountain in the country of the Mongol Khalkhas on the l. bank of the Tula, and to the E of the oorga or encampment of the Khutuktu Lama.

BAYAN KARA OOLLA, the Mongolian name of a high chain of mountains, which separates Southern Tangut from Tibet. It commences under 35° N lat., and 96° 50' E long., and directs itself towards the SE. It separates the sources of the Whangho or Hoangho from those of the Murui Ussu or the Kinsha Kiang; and is connected on the E with the Snowy mountains which border the Hoangho on the S. The Bayan Kara forms part of the great system of mountains called by the Chinese Kuen-lun, which lies to the W of the sources of the Hoangho. It is composed of three different chains; the Aklan Tsikin, the Barbukha, and the Bayan Kara, which collectively bears the Mongolian name The country in the vicinity of the of Kul-kun. last of these three mountains is an absolute desert, in which not a single habitation is found. Even in the months of August and September the Bayan Kara is nearly covered with snow, which never melts but in June or July. Wild beasts are numerous in

BAXAS (PONTA DAS), or BASHA POINT, a head- its forests. The valleys abound in good pasture and The flank of these mountains excellent water. contains mines of gold and silver. One of the highest summits of this chain is the Tchakhare Bayan Kara, a peak always covered with snow, and on which rises the Kara or Black river, which runs N, and falls into the Hoangho. The rocks of the Khadasun Tcholo, under which is the source of the Hoangho or Altun Goli, appertain to the same chain.

BAYAN OFORO, a steep mountain of Mandshuria, on the l. bank of the Sungari Ula, at the point where it issues from the barrier of palisades which separates the province of Ghirin Ula from the Mongolian tribe of Khorlos. Opposite this mountain, and 3 hours to the right of the river, is the station of Bayan Oforo.

BAYAN ULA, a mountain of Northern Turkistan, in the lands of the Middle Horde of the Kirghiz Kassaks, and to the SE of the Karagou lake. It is connected with the Ulugh Tagh. This mountain very probably contains mines of gold, silver, and copper, as fragments of these metals are frequently found upon it.
BAYARDS (LES GRANDS and LES PETITS), two villages in the Swiss cant. of Neufchatel, to the W of Boudry. The former has a pop. of 410; the latter of 200.

BAYAS, or BAIAS, a deserted town of Syria, in the pash. of Aleppo, on the bay of Iskenderun, in N lat. 36° 46', near the mouth of one of the transverse valleys of the Amanus. It is probably on the site of the Baiæ of the Romans. Williams identifies it with Myriandrus; and Pococke with Issus. A small stream of the same name flows past it; and two headlands, known as Ras B., and Eski Ras B., advance into the sea from the high shore in the neighbourhood.
BAYAT, a village of Persia, in Kuzistan, near the river Tib, in the plain of Arabistan.

BAYAZID, a city and pashalik of Asiatic Turkey, in Turkish or Western Armenia. The city is 55 E. m. SSW of Erivan, and 160 G. m. E of Erzrum, 14 m. NW of Agajek, the frontier village of Persia, and 111 m. NW of the Persian frontier; in 39° 24' N lat., 44° 43' E long., by Morier's map of Azerdbijan. It is situated in a sort of recess of a range of mountains facing Aghri Tagh, on the declivity of a mountain, on the summit of which is a strongly fortified citadel. On another rocky eminence to the left is an old castle. The houses are built partly on the slopes of the mountains, and partly in the valley. The city is surrounded with a battlemented wall and ramparts, and contains two churches, three mosques, and the pasha's palace, built on the summit of a solitary peak in the centre of the town. When visited by the missionaries Smith and Dwight, in 1830, the town had been nearly ruined by the Russians, and the bazaars were quite deserted. The inhabitants are estimated by one account at 30,000, and by another at only 10,000. So much do travellers in these regions vary in their accounts; and so fluctuating, moreover, is the pop. of territories daily exposed to the rapacious visits of banditti. All the environs of B. are abandoned to the Kurds, who come down in summer from the slopes of Mount Ararat to the very walls of the town. M. Flandin, writing in 1840, says of B., "At present there are scarcely 500 inhabited houses; commerce is dead, and the town a mere heap of ruins." A large proportion of the Armenian pop. emigrated from the place with the Russian army. The majority are Turks, and the rest Armenians. Cotton stuffs are fabricated here.—The district and city of Bayazid are under the government of a pasha of two tails as a dependence on Erzrum. It seems to correspond to the Basenia of Moses of Chorene.

BAYDON, a parish in Wiltshire, 41 m. N of Ramsbury. Area 3,060 acres. Pop. 335. PARKATA PARKA PARKA PARKA PARKA PARKA PARKA PARKA PARKA PARKA PARKA PARKA PARKA PARKA PARKA PARKA PARKA PARKA PARKA PARKA PARKA PARKA PARKA PARKA PARKA PARKA PARKA PARKA PARKA PARKA PARKA PARKA PARKA PARKA PARKA PARKA PARK BAY
BAYE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Marne, cant. of Montmort. Pop. 690.
BAYEL, a commune of France, in the dep. of Aube, cant. and 4 m. from Bar-sur-Aube. Pop. 617.
BAYENGHEM, a commune of France, in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais, cant. of Lumbres. Pop. 271.
BAYERBACH, a town of Austria, in the Hausruck circle, SW of Aschach.
BAYERSDORF. See BAIERSDORF.

BAYERWALD, a branch of the Böhmerwaldgebirge, or Bohemian Forest mountains, extending from Mount Rachel to Ratisbon, and separating a portion of the basin of the Danube from that of the Regen. Its formation is chiefly granitic; and its average alt. 2,000 ft. above sea-level.
BAYET, a commune of France, in the dep. of Allier, cant. of Saint Pourcain. Pop. 1,069.

BAYEUX, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Calvados.—The arrond. has an area of 94,412 hectares. Pop. in 1836, 81,244; in 1846, 80,732. It comprises the 6 cantons of Balleroy, B., Caumont, Isigny, Ryes, and Trésières.—The cant. comprises 17 com. Pop. in 1831, 15,381.—The com. is 16 m. NW of Caen, on the Aure, 5 m. above its embouchure. Pop. in 1836, 9,676; in 1846, 9,106. The town is an ancient and ill-built place, most of the houses having mere wood and plaster frames. The cathedral is said to be the oldest place of worship in Normandy. The chief articles of trade are serge, hosiery, lace, grain, cyder, and butter. In the hotel of the prefecture of B., in a gallery built for its exhibition in 1839, is preserved the famous B. tapestry, a roll of linen canvass 214 ft. long, and 20 in. wide, on which are represented the different events connected with the conquest of England by the Normans; and of which a minute account is given in the 4th vol. of the Vetusta Monumenta, and in the Penny Cyclopedia, vol. iv.—B. was the Augustodurus and Bodrocassium of the Romans; the Baiex, Baieves, or Baiox of the Middle ages. It was repeatedly devastated by fire, and was taken by the English in 1450.

BAYFIELD, a parish in Norfolk, 2 m. NW of Holt. Area returned with Glandford p. Pop. 21.— Also a v. in Upper Canada, at the entrance of a stream of the same name into Lake Huron, 12 m. below Goderich. Pop. 150.—The river B. takes its rise in the great swamp N of the Huron tract.
BAYFORD, a parish in Hertfordshire, 4 m. SSE of Hertford. Area 1,450 acres. Pop. 357.

BAYHAM, a hamlet in the p. of Frant, Sussex, 2 m. W of Lamberhurst.—Also a township of Upper Canada, in the London district; bounded on the S by Lake Erie, and watered by Big Otter creek. Pop. in 1842, 2,250.
BAYLA. See Bela.

BAYLE'N, a town of Spain, in Andalusia, in the prov. of Jaen, in N lat. 38° 2', at the foot of the Sierra Morena. Pop. 4,796. It has manufactures of oil, glass, and soap. B. is celebrated in the annals of the late peninsular war, for an event which contributed greatly to animate the courage of Spain against its French invaders. Joseph Bonaparte had entered Madrid as king; the provinces of Leon, Valencia, Valladolid, Zamora, and Salamanca, had been subdued and disarmed; in the south alone, on the Guadalquivir, in the naturally fortified Andalusia, in Cordova, Grenada, Jaen, the spirit of insurrection still prevailed, and was excited as much as possible by the junta of Seville. Thither General Dupont directed his march, at the end of May, with three divisions. Cordova and Jaen were taken by assault, after the most terrible resistance. The corps of Castannos soon increased to 30,000 men. The able manœuvres of this general. together with famine and sickness in the French army, augmented by the total want of hospitals, prepared the way for the overthrow of Dupont. In a short time, 3,000 Spaniards had possession of the Sierra Morena, in the rear of his army. In order to re-establish his communication with the capital, he occupied the cities of B. and Carolina with detachments, while he himself took a position near Andujar, on the Guadalquivir. But, on the 14th of July, 18,000 men, with some pieces of heavy artillery, marched against the front of the French position near Andujar; while 3,000 men came through the defiles of the Sierra Morena upon the rear, and 6,000 men attacked Dupont's left wing. He defended himself for three days with skill and courage; but the 18th of July decided the contest. The Spanish generals, Reding and Compigny, attacked B., while Pennas and Jones overawed the main body, under Dupont, who was compelled to evacuate Andujar, after B. had been taken by the Spaniards. The action had continued nine hours, when Dupont requested a suspension of arms, but was told that he must surrender at discretion. Meanwhile the division of Vedel, not acquainted with the proceedings of Dupont, had attacked the Spaniards anew, and taken the regiment of Cordova prisoners, together with two pieces of artillery, but were finally overpowered by superior numbers. On the 23d of July, the whole French army, 17,000 men strong, being surrounded, was obliged to capitulate, having lost 3,000 men on the field of battle. The divisions of Dupont and Vedel were made prisoners of war; the latter was to be permitted to embark at Cadiz for Rochefort; the same terms were afterwards promised to the division of Dupont, but not fulfilled. General Dupont returned, with his staff, to France, and was arrested at Toulon, and subjected to a trial. But, before a decision, he was delivered by the capture of Paris, March 30, 1814. He was afterwards appointed, by Louis XVIII., minister of war; but was superseded by Soult, in December 1814.
BAYLEY (POINT), a headland on the N coast of Australia, in S lat. 16° 35′ 10", long. 6° 55′ 30" E of Port Essington.
BAYLHAM, a parish in Suffolk, 3 m. S by E of Needham. Area 830 acres. Pop. 275.
BAYON, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Meurthe. The com. is 10 m. SW of Luneville, on the r. bank of the Moselle. Pop. 891.—The cant. comprises 27 com. Pop. in 1831, 10,545.
BAYONA, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Madrid, 20 m. SSE of Madrid, at the confluence of the Jarama and Tajuna.—Also a seaport of Spain, in Galicia, 9 m. SW of Vigo. Pop. 1,719.
BAYONA ISLES, two small islands, with a number of insular rocks, off the coast of Galicia, at the entrance of the bay of Bayona, 6 m. NNW of Bayona.

BAYONNE, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Basses-Pyrénées. —The arrond. has an area of 106,256 hectares; and comprises the 7 cant. of B., Bidache, Espelette, Hasparren, Saint-Jean-de-Luz, La Bastide-Clairence, and Ustarits. Pop. in 1836, 84,519; in 1846, 80,912. -The cant., comprising 9 com., had a pop. in 1831 of 23,518.—The com. and town of B. are situated in N lat. 43° 30', on the confluence of the Adour and the Nive, whose united waters fall into the bay of Biscay, 3½ m. below B. These rivers divide the town into three sections: that on the l. bank of the Nive is called Le Grand-B., or the Large town; that on the r. bank of the Nive, or between the Nive and the Adour, is called Le Petit-B., or the Small town; and these two divisions form the com. of B.—The third division, composing the faubourg of Saint Esprit, a commune in the dep. of Landes, lies on the r. bank of the Adour; and in this suburb is the citadel, which commands both the harbour and the town. Great and Little B. have also each a castle and bastions; and are protected on the land-side by a wall and redoubts. B. is a well-built commercial city, conducting an extensive commerce with Spain. Its most important exports are wood, wine, brandy, leather, cloth, and hams; and it possesses about one-eleventh part of the transit-trade of the kingdom. Its finest building is the cathedral, a small but elegant Gothic edifice of the 13th cent. The Place de Grammont is finely built; and the quay affords a fine promenade. The principal manufactures of B. are those of hams and chocolate,-for both of which it has long been celebrated,-cream-of-tartar, and the liqueur called Andaye. Ship-building is also actively engaged in; and the government has magazines, workshops, and slips here. The shifting sand-bank at the mouth of the Adour forms a great obstacle to its commercial prosperity. The Nive is navigable for about 30 m. from its mouth; and the Adour for 70. By them the BAZ timber, tar, and iron of the Pyrenees are conveyed to the coast; and their estuary forms a harbour capable of receiving vessels of 50 guns. The pop. in 1846 amounted to 15,322.—D'Anville considers B. to be the ancient Lapurdum. In 1564 Catherine de Medici, and the duke of Alba, had an interview in B. at which, it is generally supposed, the massacre of the Huguenots was first devised. Here Charles IV. of Spain, and the prince of Asturias, afterwards Ferdinand VII., were arrested by Napoleon in 1808, and made to transfer their rights in Spain and India to the French emperor by a formal deed; and here too, in the same year, Napoleon convened a Spanish junta to draw up, or rather to receive from his hands, a so-called constitution for their country, which was published on the 6th of July. When the British forces under Wellington entered the French territory in 1814, the citadel of B. was invested by them; but several days after Napoleon had abdicated the French made a sortie on the British lines which was only repulsed with considerable loss.

BAYONNE, a river of Lower Canada, in the co. of Berthier, which flows into the St. Lawrence near a v. of the same name, opposite Isle Castor. It is navigable by loaded boats for 4 or 5 m. from its mouth; but its upper navigation is prevented by rapids and falls, two of which are 16 ft. perpendicular.
BAYONS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Basses-Alpes, cant. of Turriers. Pop. 800.
BAYONVILLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Meurthe, cant. of Thiaucourt.
BAYONVILLERS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Somme, cant. of Rozieres. Pop. 380.
BAYPORE, a town of Hindostan, on the Malabar coast, 8 m. S of Calicut. Tippoo Saib attempted to found a city and construct an arsenal here; but it is a place of little importance.
BAYREUTH. See BAIREUTH.
BAYS. See Bais.
BAYSAYE, a town of Siam, on the gulf of Siam, in N lat. 11° 29'.
BAYSE. See Baise.
BAYSWATER, a hamlet in the p. of Paddington, Middlesex, 3 m. W of London, on the Uxbridge road.
BAYTON, a parish in Worcestershire, 6 m. W by S of Bewdley. Area 1,960 acres. Pop. 468.

BAYUDAH, an extensive desert tract in Kordofan, inhabited by Kababish Arabs. It is generally crossed between Ambukol and El Hajir in six or seven days. Its surface is prevailingly flat, with a few low hills. It abounds in herbaceous plants, with one or two varieties of the mimosa. The leopard, oryx, and gazelle, are found in it.
BAYUGO, a town on the NE coast of Mindanao. in the Philippine group.
BAYUNAHS, or Bajunahs, a tribe on the coast of Zanguebar, to the S of the Juba river, whose language is said to be a compound of Galla and Somauli.
BAYVIL, a parish in Pembrokeshire, 2 m. N of Newport. Pop. 130.

BA'ZA, a city of Spain, in Andalusia, in the prov. and 60 m. ENE of Granada, in N lat. 37° 30', near the N base of the E termination of the Sierra de B., a high ridge of transition limestone, extending from Guadiz to B. Pop. 7,000. It is a bishop's see, and has a cathedral, and several ancient churches. The hoya or valley of B. is fertile in grain, flax, hemp, and fruit; and renowned for its red wines. The geological features of the surrounding country have been described by Colonel Silvertop in a communication to the Geological Society. - B. is the Roman Basti.

BAZADOIS, an ancient district of France, in Guyenne, the cap. of which was Bazas; and the chief towns Langon, La Reole, Caumont, and Castelgeloux. Area 326,990 hectares, of which 213,353 are now comprised in the dep. of La Gironde; and 116,637 in that of Lot-et-Garonne.
BAZALETHI, a district of Asiatic Russia, in the prov. of Georgia, on the r. bank of the Aragui. It is fertile in grain, barley, and millet.
BAZALIA, a village of Russia, in the gov. of Volhynia, in the district and 30 m. W of Staro- Konstantinof. Pop. 350.
BAZANCOURT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Marne, cant. of Bourgogne, 10 m. NNE of Reims. Pop. 931.
BAZANOW, a town of Russian Poland, in the gov. of Podlachia, circle of Lukow. Pop. 400
BAZARGAHI-TEDID, or Bazaria, a village of Romelia, in the sanjak of Salonica, 1 day's journey from Siderehkapsi, 24 m. E of Salonica.
BAZARJIK, or Haji Oghli Bazar, a town and district of Romelia, in the sanjak of Silistria, 30 m. N by W of Varna.
BAZARKHAN, a town of Anatolia, in the sanjak of Mentesha, on the road from Dauas to Denizli, and at the S base of the Baba-dagh. It is 30 m. S of Denizli.
BAZARKOI, a village and kadilik of Anatolia, in the sanjak of Koja-ili, 15 m. W of Iznik. Pop. 1,100.
BAZAR-NOVI. See Novibazar.

BAZARUTA, an island off the coast of Sofala, in the Mozambique channel, in S lat. 21° 34', E long. 35° 29', N of the cape of the same name. BAZARUTA (CAPE), OF CAPE SAINT SEBASTIAN, a remarkable promontory on the SE coast of Africa, in S lat. 21° 28', E long. 35° 29'. The famous pearlfishery of Sofala was off this point of coast.

BAZAS, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of La Gironde.- The arrond. comprises the 7 cant. of Auros, B., Captieux, Grignols, Laugon, Saint-Symphorien, and Villandraux. Area 154,598 hectares. Pop. in 1836, 53,721; in 1846, 55,480.—The cant. comprises 13 com. Pop. in 1831, 11,000. - The commune and town are 41 m. SSE of Bordeaux, on the Beauve, an affluent of the Garonne, in N lat. 41° 25' 57", W 248. long. 0° 12′ 52". Pop. in 1846, 4,407. It has a fine Gothic cathedral. Its principal manufactures are leather, druggets, saltpetre, and wax candles. It is supposed to occupy the site of the Roman Vasatæ.
BAZEILLE (SAINTE), a commune of France, in the dep. of Lot-et-Garonne, cant. and 4 m. WNW of Marmande, near the r. bank of the Garonne. Pop. 2,798.
BAZEILLES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ardennes, cant. and 3 m. from Sedan. Pop. 1,380. There are extensive iron-works here.
BAZELE, a commune of Belgium, in E Flanders, 22 m. NE of Termonde, on the Scheld. Pop. 4,400. An immense quantity of bricks are annually manufactured here.
BAZETTA, a township of Trumbull co., in the state of Ohio, U. S., 5 m. E of Warren. It is a fine farming district.
BAZIEGE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Haute-Garonne, cant. of Montgiscard. Pop. 1,695.
BAZILE (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Ardeche, cant. of La Mastre. Pop. 1,138.
BAZOCHE-GOUET (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of Eure-et-Loir, cant. of Authon, 15 m. SE of Nogent-le-Rotrou. Pop. 2,137.
BAZOCHES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Aisne, cant. of Braisne, on the Vêle. Pop. 345. It is on the site of the ancient Basilica.
BAZOCHES-LES-GALLERANDES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Loiret, cant. of Outarville, 20 m. NNE of Orleans. Pop. 1,107.

BAZOCHES-SUR-HOENE, a canton and com- 1,732. The town is said to derive its name from its BEA mune of France, in the dep. of Orne.—The cant. comprises 12 com. Pop. in 1831, 7,718.—The com., 4 m. NW of Mortagne, has a pop. of 1,615.
BAZOCHES-AU-HOLME, a commune of France, in the dep. of Orne, cant. of Putanges, 10 m. NW of Argentau. Pop. 1,387.
BAZOGE (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of Sarthe, cant. and 6 m. NNW of Le Mans. Pop. 2,323. There are iron mines here.
BAZOGES-EN-PAREDS, a commune of France, in Vendée, in the cant. of La Chataigneraie. Pop. 1,748.
BAZOILLE-SUR-MEUSE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Vosges, cant. and 4 m. SSW of Neufchateau. Pop. 585.
BAZOUGE-DE-CHEMERE' (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of Mayenne, cant. of Meslay, on the Vaige, 15 m. ESE of Laval. Pop. 1,200.
BAZOUGERS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Mayenne, cant. of Meslay, 9 m. ESE of Laval. Pop. 1,390.
BAZOUGES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Mayenne, cant. of Chateau-Gontier. Pop. 1,537.
BAZOUGES - DU - DE'SERT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ille-et-Vilaine, cant. of Louvigné-du-Desert, 8 m. NNE of Fougeres. Pop. 2,076. There are paper and glass-works here.
BAZOUGES-SOUS-HE'DE', a commune of France, in the dep. of Ille-et-Vilaine, cant. of Hede. Pop. 934.
BAZOUGES-SUR-LE-LOIR, a commune of France, in the dep. of Sarthe, cant. and 4 m. W of La Fleche, on the Loir. Pop. 1,823.
BAZOUGES-LA-PEROUSE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ille-et-Vilaine, cant. of Antrain, 20 m. NW of Fougeres. Pop. 4,500.
BAZUEL, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nord, cant. of Le Cateau. Pop. 1,112.
BAZZANO, a town in the States-of-the-Church, 12 m. W of Bologna, on the Samoggia. Pop. 3,000.
BEACHAMPTON, a parish in Buckinghamshire, 5 m. NE of Buckingham. Area 2,110 acres. Pop.
BEACHAMWELL, a parish in Norfolk, 5 m. WSW of Swaffham. Area 3,730 acres. Pop. 246.
BEACHLEY, a hamlet in Tidenham p., Gloucestershire, 3 m. SSE of Chepston, at the confluence of the Wye with the Severn. Pop. 224.
BEACHVILLE, a village in the township of West Oxford, Upper Canada, on the E branch of the Thames. Pop. 300.

BEACHY-HEAD, a promontory on the coast of Sussex, between Hastings and Seaford, and about 14 m. S of Eastbourn, in N lat. 50° 44', E long. 0° 13'. This is the highest headland on the southern coast of England, and is frequently the scene of shipwrecks in bad weather. A lighthouse was erected here in 1828. The light is revolving, and may be seen at the distance of 28 m. in clear weather. The rock rises in many places almost perpendicularly from the water, and is hollowed out into caverns, which are the resort of multitudes of sea-fowl. The country between this place and Arundel rises into the hills called the South Downs. On the 30th of June, 1690, the combined Dutch and English fleets were defeated within sight of this promontory, by a French armament of superior force.
BEACON HILLS, a mountain-range of Wales, in Brecknockshire, a part of the Black Mountain chain. They are of abrupt outline, and attain an alt. of 2,862 ft.

BEACONSFIELD, a parish and market-town in Buckinghamshire, 23 m. W by N of London, 36 m. SE of Buckingham. Area of p. 3,710 acres. Pop. 2 R being built on an eminence on which beacon-fires were formerly lighted. It consists of four streets, which meet in a spacious central market-place. The principal street is on the high road from Uxbridge to High Wycombe. The trade of the town arises chiefly from its being the market-town of a large agricultural district.—In its church the poet Waller, and the politician Edmund Burke, are buried.
BEACUL, or BYAKUL, a town of India, on the Malabar coast, in N lat. 12° 23'. It stands on a promontory projecting into the sea, defended by a fort, and forming a bay to the N. It is a small place.
BEADLAM, a township in the p. of Kirkdale, N. R. of Yorkshire, 3 m. E of Helmsley. Area 620 acres. Pop. 158.
BEADNELL, a chapelry in the p. of Bamborough, Northumberland, 9 m. SE of Belford, on the coast. Pop. 323. It has a small harbour.
BEAFORD, a parish in Devonshire, 5 m. SE of Great Torrington. Area 3,760 acres. Pop. 713.
BEAGE (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of Ardeche, cant. of Montpezat. Pop. 2,406.
BEAGHALL, or BEAL, a township in Killington p., W. R. of Yorkshire. Area 1,570 acres. Pop. 568.

BEAGLE BANK, a bank of white sand and dead coral, off the coast of Australia, in S lat. 15° 20', E long. 123° 36'. It rises 15 ft. above the mean level of the water. A reef runs out from it 21 m. in a NNW, and another 1 m. in a SSE direction.—Stokes.
BEAGLE BAY, a bay on the NW coast of Australia, in S lat. 16° 50'. It affords the best anchorage on the coast, but is exposed to W winds.
BEAGLE BLUFF, a headland on the coast of the Santa Cruz river, on the E coast of Patagonia, 25 m. up the river.

BEAGLE CHANNEL, a narrow channel on the coast of Tierra del Fuego, to the N of Lennox island. It runs 120 m. in nearly a direct line between high ranges of snowy mountains. Its average width is 11/2 m. There are numerous islets and rocks in it.—King.
BEAGLE ISLAND, a small high rocky island off the coast of Australia, in S lat. 34° 49', E long. of Swan river 19° 4'.—Also an island off Cape Isabel, on the SW coast of Patagonia.
BEAKSBOURNE, a parish in Kent, 3½ m. ESE of Canterbury. Area 1,200 acres. Pop. 332.
BEAK'S KAY, an islet off the great Bahama bank, in N lat. 25° 22′, W long. 79° 13′.
BEAL, a small river in Kent, rising near Shadoxhurst, and flowing NW to the Medway, into which it falls near Talding.
BEALINGS (GREAT and LITTLE), two adjoining parishes in Suffolk, 2½ m. and 3 m. W of Woodbridge. Area of Great B. 830 acres. Pop. 377. Area of Little B. 410 acres. Pop. 322.
BEALNABRACK, a small stream in co. Galway, flowing by a SSE course into the head of an elongated bay of Lough Corrib.
BEALSVILLE, a village in Monroe co., in the state of Ohio, U.S., 127 m. ESE of Columbus. Pop. 100.—Also a village in Washington co., Pennsylvania.

BEAMINSTER, a parish and market-town in Dorsetshire, 137 m. WSW of London, and 16 m. WNW of Dorchester. The p., with the tything of Langdon, has an area of 4,350 acres. Pop. 3,270.— The town is neatly built. It possesses manufactories of sail-cloth, coarse pottery, and tin, iron, and copper ware, besides two or three flax-mills. It is one of the polling-places for the county members.
BEAMISH, a township in the chapelry of Tanfield, p. of Chester-le-Street, Durham, 6 m. SW of Gateshead. Area 4,120 acres. Pop. 2,000.
BEAMORE, a village in the p. of Colpe, co. Meath, 2 m. S of Drogheda. Pop. in 1831, 123.
BEAMSLEY, a township in the parishes of Ad- BEA dingham and Skipton, in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 6 m. E by N of Skipton. Area 1,820 acres. Pop. 235.
BEAMSVILLE, a village in the township of Clinton, in Upper Canada, 22 m. from Hamilton. Pop. 250.
BEANE, a river of Hertfordshire, rising in Yardley p., and flowing past Walton and Stapleford to the Lea near Hoddesdon.
BEANLEY, a township in the p. of Edingham, Northumberland, 7 m. NW of Alnwick. Pop. 176.
BEAR. See BERE.

BEAR (CAPE), a headland on the coast of the island of Anticosti, in the St. Lawrence, in N lat. 49° 35'.—Also a cape on the coast of the N Polar sea, in N lat. 69° 41', E long. 162° 14', forming the E point of the mouth of the Kolyma.
